result:
I keep thinking about the moment when the wind changes direction.

Not metaphorically—literally, physically, the small, almost invisible pivot when the air that was sliding across your cheek suddenly decides to turn around and go home. You feel it first at the hairline, a cool thread that slips under the strands and lifts them like a question. Then it’s on your neck, the underside of your forearm, the thin skin at your wrist where the pulse keeps its own anxious time. The whole afternoon rearranges itself without asking permission: the oak that had been whispering secrets to the window screen now answers some other tree you can’t even see; the smell of charcoal and rosemary drifts away from you like a party you weren’t invited to after all.  

There is no announcement, no bell rung in the belfry of the sky. One moment the world is persuading itself it knows how the story ends; the next, an unseen hinge creaks and everything drafts sideways. I love that small treason. It reminds me how much of what we call “weather” is actually conspiracy: a billion molecules voting with their feet, defecting, forming new coalitions in the upper corridors of the troposphere. They don’t care about your picnic, your laundry on the line, the elaborate hairdo you constructed in the mirror like a small, hopeful empire. They care about pressure, about gradient, about the vast, abstract mathematics of heat wanting to be somewhere else.  

I think we should all be allowed one gratuitous change of direction per year, no questions asked. You wake up, feel the invisible hairline shift, and simply walk out of the life you’ve been inhabiting—job, relationship, city, favorite brand of toothpaste—into whatever is now downwind. No moral homework, no debriefing, no sad custody negotiations over the houseplants. Just: the wind turned, and I turned with it. People would greet each other differently: “Hi, Janet. Still the same quadrant?” “No, I tacked east at breakfast. My mother thinks I’m in Cleveland, but the pressure dropped and here I am, learning to make congee on a portable burner in an unfamiliar kitchen.” We’d keep a communal drawer of abandoned loyalty cards, half-knitted scarves, almost-finished degrees, the way coastal pubs stock lost oilskins for whoever the tide brings next.  

Of course we do it anyway, in smaller, sanctioned doses: dye the hair, quit the app, swear off sugar, ghost the group chat. But those are declarations, press releases. I’m talking about the wordless pivot, the quiet lift of the hairline, the moment before language has had time to insist on a story. Children know it—they’ll be digging a moat one second and sprinting across the dunes the next, summoned by nothing you can detect. Then we train them to finish the moat, to sign the permission slip, to stay until the credits roll. We call it growing up, but maybe it’s just growing still, the way a tree grows still—rings tightening around the heart until the heart can’t feel the wind shift anymore.  

Tonight the oak outside my desk is already in motion, leaves showing their pale undersides like a flock of hands surrendering. Somewhere above them the jet stream is plotting a coup against yesterday’s weather map. I have laundry on the line, and a half-written sentence that thinks it knows where it’s going. I’m waiting for the cool thread, the small treason, the moment the world leans in and says, Run.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
